questions.question: What is a blockchain, most simply?
options: A centralized database run by one company
An append-only chain of cryptographically linked blocks
A type of cloud storage bucket

questions.question: What does it mean for a blockchain to be 'immutable'?
options: Anyone can edit past records freely
Confirmed records are extremely hard to alter retroactively
The chain never adds new blocks

questions.question: What is the role of a 'consensus mechanism'?
options: It compresses block data to save space
It lets nodes agree on the valid state of the ledger

questions.question: In Proof of Work, what are miners primarily competing to do?
options: Guess a hash below a target difficulty
Vote on which transactions are pretty

questions.question: What distinguishes Proof of Stake from Proof of Work?
options: Validators are chosen based on staked capital, not raw computation
It requires no network at all

questions.question: What is a 'block' in a blockchain?
options: A bundle of transactions committed together

questions.question: What problem does a blockchain's consensus solve that a normal database does not need to?
options: Agreement among mutually-distrusting parties without a central authority

questions.question: What is a 'fork' in blockchain terms?
options: A divergence in the chain's history or protocol rules

questions.question: Why is a blockchain often described as 'decentralized'?
options: It runs on one giant supercomputer
Its ledger is maintained by many independent nodes
It is owned by a single bank

questions.question: What does 'finality' mean for a transaction?
options: The point at which it is considered irreversible
The moment it is first broadcast
When it is deleted from the mempool

questions.question: What is a private key used for?
options: Publicly identifying your account to others

questions.question: Which of these is safe to share publicly?
What is a seed phrase (mnemonic)?
options: A human-readable backup that can regenerate your keys
A public username for your wallet

questions.question: What is the practical meaning of 'not your keys, not your coins'?
options: If a third party holds your keys, they control your funds
You must mint your own coins
Keys are optional on most chains

questions.question: How is a public address typically derived?
options: From the public key via hashing/encoding
It is identical to the private key

questions.question: What is a 'cold wallet'?
options: A wallet kept offline for security
A wallet with a negative balance
A wallet that only holds stablecoins
A wallet that has been frozen by a bank

questions.question: What does a digital signature prove about a transaction?
options: That the holder of the private key authorized it
That the fee was the highest possible
That it was sent on a weekday

questions.question: If you lose your seed phrase and have no other backup, what typically happens?
options: Your coins move to a recovery pool

questions.question: On Usernode, what prefix do wallet addresses use?
On Usernode, a wallet address is the bech32 of what?
What is the Usernode Dapps homepage primarily for?
options: Listing dapps built on the Usernode chain

questions.question: How does the Usernode Dapps homepage decorate each dapp row with stats?
options: By deriving unique senders and tx counts from the explorer
By asking each dapp's owner manually
By reading the user's local files

questions.question: In the Submit-a-dapp flow, what confirms a new dapp gets published?
options: A confirmed on-chain fee payment to the Community Fund Reserve
A moderator approving it by email

questions.question: How are usernames resolved per wallet on Usernode (as used by the homepage)?
options: Latest on-chain set_username transaction per sender wins

questions.question: Which of these is a real dapp listed on the Usernode homepage?
Usernode apps commonly run as child apps inside which platform?
Why does the homepage query the explorer with 'recipient: pubkey' for stats?
options: To reflect what each dapp actually received (excluding sender change)

questions.question: What does 'HODL' originate from and commonly mean?
options: A misspelling of 'hold' — to keep an asset long term

questions.question: What is an 'airdrop' in crypto?
options: A free distribution of tokens to eligible wallets

questions.question: What does 'gas' usually refer to?
options: The fee paid to process a transaction

questions.question: What is a 'smart contract'?
options: A legal PDF signed by lawyers
Code deployed on-chain that runs deterministically
A customer agreement with an exchange

questions.question: What does 'DeFi' stand for?
What is an NFT, at its core?
options: A token representing unique, non-interchangeable ownership
A new fiat token issued by banks

questions.question: What does 'DYOR' advise?
options: Do Your Own Research before investing

questions.question: What is a 'rug pull'?
options: A scam where creators abandon a project and take the funds
